History.] C. was discovered in 1492, by Christopher Columbus. In 1511, Don Diego Velasquez sailed from St. Domingo, with four vessels and about 300 men, for the conquest of the island. He landed on the 25th of July, near the bay of St. Iago, to which he gave its name. The natives, commanded by the cacique Hatuey, who had fled from St. Domingo, his native country, on account of the cruelties of the Spaniards, in vain endeavoured to oppose the progress of the invaders. The noise of the fire-arms was sufficient to disperse the poor Indians. Hatuey was taken prisoner and condemned to be burned alive, which sentence was executed after he had refused to be baptized. This diabolical act filled all the other caciques with terror; and they hastened to pay homage to Velasquez, who met with no more opposition. The conquest of C. did not cost the Spaniards a single man. The conquerors, not finding the mines sufficiently rich to induce them to work them, gradually exterminated the natives, whom they could not employ. After the conquest of C., more than two centuries elapsed without the occurrence of any memorable incident. In 1741, the English admiral Vernon sailed, in July, from Jamaica, and entered the bay of Guantanamo, which he named Cumberland. He landed his troops 20 m. up the river, where they remained in perfect inaction until November, when they returned to Jamaica. Notwithstanding the disastrons termination of this expedition, the English government did not relinquish the idea of taking possession of C. In 1762, they sent from England a formidable expedition, which, after its junction with the naval force which had been already serving in the West Indies, consisted of 19 ships-of-the-line, 18 small vessels-of-war, and 150 transports, which conveyed 12,000 troops. The whole of this fleet appeared off Havana on the 6th of June; and 4,000 more troops went from N America in July to reinforce them. The Spaniards used every effort to defend the city. The English were several times repelled, but at last the Spaniards surrendered on the 13th of August. The booty obtained by the English was great. About 3,000,000 drs. in specie, and a large quantity of goods, fell into their hands, besides a great quantity of munitions of war, 9 ships-of-the-line, and 4 frigates. In 1763, the conquerors, notwithstanding the high opinion that they had of the importance of C., restored it to Spain, in exchange for the Floridas. It has long been sufficiently notorious that the possession of this noble island was an object of eager desire to a large and unscrupulous party in the United States. The advantages which the annexation of C. would confer upon the Union were thus set forth in the New York Sun, in July 1847:- "When in Havana last winter, we had a consultation with a meeting of the most influential and wealthy men of that city, upon the union of C. to the United States, and promised to lay the matter before the people of this country as soon as the Mexican question had been disposed of. Mexico, to all intents and purposes, is now in our possession. All parties in the United States, and even foreign nations, look upon that question as settled, so far as conquest and occupation are concerned, and we now hasten to fulfil our promise, and that promise must be our apology for this article. C., by geographical position, necessity, and right, belongs to the United States; it may and must be ours. The moment has arrived to place it in our hands, and under our flag. C. is in the market for sale, and we are authorised, by parties eminently able to fulfil what they propose, to say, that if the United States will offer the Spanish government 100,000,000 dollars, C. is ours; and that, with one week's notice, the whole amount will be raised and paid over by the inhabitants of the island. One week is all they ask, if our government will only make the offer for them to act upon, and which Spain is ready to accept. This is no vision, but a fixed fact, of which we have seen and now hold the most undoubted proofs. The possession of C. will complete our chain of territory, and give us the North American continent. It is the garden of the world, -the key to the Gulf, -and the richest spot of its size on the face of the earth. From Florida-point to its NE coast is only 50 m., bringing the island almost within cannonshot and sight of the United States. C. yields to Spain an annual revenue of 7,000,000 dollars, besides supporting 25,000 soldiers, expending at least 1,000,000 a-year upon fortifications, and paying another 1,000,000 in salaries to civil and military officers; in fact, her total revenue to Spain is over 10,000,000, which will double, the moment it comes under the United States. C. produces the best sugar, coffee, tobacco, and tropical fruits, in the world. It has gold and silver, and the richest copper-mines known. Its harbours, surface of the country, and climate, are unrivalled; and in less than five years its revenue would more than pay the entire purchase-money asked by Spain. C. must be ours! Stretching from Florida to Yucatan, it commands the Gulf and coast of Mexico. It is larger than Ireland, nearly as large as all the New England States, containing 43,500 sq. m. of the most fertile soil under the sun. To us it is indispensable. We want its harbours for our ships to touch at to and from Mexico, for the accommodation of American and English transatlantic steamers, for its products and trade, and as the grand key to the gulf of Mexico. Give us C., and our possessions are complete. Spain is ready to sell, the Cubans are waiting for us to make the purchase, and to come at once into the Union. Let the thing be done; we pledge ourselves that 100,000,000 will be forthcoming to meet the offer, and that C. will prove the richest conquest ever made by the United States." It does not appear from the issue of events thus far that the Cubans have felt any particular desire to renounce their old allegiance for a connexion with the United States; although such a desire has doubtless been fostered by resident Americans who have either by purchase or mortgage become the virtual owners of many of the plantations in C.; and the journalists of the slaveholding states of the Union have laboured unremittingly to promote an armed expedition against C. and to induce the Cubans to "issue their pronunciamento, and commence their revolution." The piratical descent of Narciso Lopez and a band of adventurers organised in the States, upon the coasts of C., in May 1850, was met and crushed in a manner which clearly proved that all classes of Cubans were fully disposed to support their own government against overt or insidious aggressions on its rights. Whether, however, Spain shall secure to herself the permanent retention of this important island for many years will in a great measure depend upon the discretion and judgment evinced by the cabinet of Madrid at this juncture. If the memorial of grievances, to which allusion has already been made, and which is understood to occupy the attention of the Spanish ministry at the present moment, shall ensure such a reform in the administration as the Cubans demand, it is not improbable that C. will remain for years the richest jewel in the Crown of Spain. But if-in defiance alike of justice and sound policy-the well-founded remonstrances of the Cubans shall be treated with neglect, a very few years will witness the commencement of a revolutionary struggle in which Spain will have enlisted against herself the feelings as well as the judgment of all civilized society, and the issue of which therefore cannot for a moment be doubtful. Our fears, we confess, are stronger than our hopes; but we heartily concur in the followng remarks on this subject by an able and far-seeing journalist. "If ever there was a colony with the elements of greatness it is Cuba. As the colony of a tropical climate,—its wealth produced by the labour of the Negro slave,—it stands alone in that great condition both of civilization and security:-its white pop. exceeds its black. Of the million souls in C. more than one-half are of Spanish origin, which shows that it contains not merely a caste of proprietors, but a middle class of industrious men of European descent, and even of labourers. This great mass of pop. has sprung up, too, within little more than half-a-century; and in consequence of the commercial freedom which the political convulsions of Europe procured for C., in despite of the ignorant tyranny of the mother-country, the extensive fertility of the island,-its position.-its requirements,-its produce,-the facilities of communication by sea and land which it possesses, -have all contributed to render it the true Eldorado, sought by so many adventurers, and found by none. The most magnificent dependency that could have been given to a European country was C. And Spain has more facilities for keeping it than any other country can have for its western colonies. Its half-million of the Spanish race, in the midst of a Negro population nearly equal in amount, would alone have been a strong link to bind it to the mothercountry. Menaced by the ambition of the people of the United States, it might, as portion of an old European monarchy, and of the old European system, claim the protection not only of Spain, but of other countries allied with Spain, and interested in the independence of these western islands. But, with all this, C. is an important, and more than a self-subsistent land. It is no little barque, to be towed after a crazy old vessel. It has wider necessities and facilities of trade: and as to Spain imposing a head-tax upon it—as it has had the folly to do—the very idea is intolerable and preposterous. The half-million of the Spanish race in C., too, must have some show of self-government. To suppose that an island situated between Jamaica, San Domingo, and Florida, should rest contented with the stupid tyranny of a military viceroy, responsible to no one, is insanity. But the moderado generals and statesmen, who govern Spain, have no idea of conceding to popular necessities. Repression, resistance, martial law, executions, these form their sole maxim of politics. We know not how long they may rule Spain with such a rod, but C. they will decidedly lose, if they go on with their stupid conservatism. It is difficult to say whether the rule of such men as Roncali, O'Donnell, and Tacon, is more disgusting to the Spanish pop. of C. or to the black pop. The worst form of slavery exists in the farms of C.; the most miserable and punctilious tyranny disgusts the European race of the Havannah. Unless some politicians, liberal and wise, attain to power in Madrid, and apply themselves to change the entire administration of C., it is decidedly lost to Spain. It does not, however, follow, that because it shakes off the yoke of Spain, it is to put on that of the Anglo-Saxon race of the United States. The Whites of C. have no need of the Federation to protect them against the Negroes. Nor have they need of any such aid to protect them from too sudden emancipation. They form a powerful body, wealthy enough to support a large army. They have every material of self-government and self-defence. C. is not, like other islands in the same latitude, exploité by speculative foreigners or sojourners on the island. Its proprietors live on it, increase in it, and have their progeny to provide for in connexion with the island, not in connexion with such sums as may be extracted from it. Such a body, with such interests and views, would soon see the necessity of stopping the influx of any more savages from Africa. And with the prohibition of that trade would commence a very different treatment of the slaves, and steps towards their final emancipation." [Daily News.] Authorities.] Historia de Cuba. Boston, 8vo., 1829. — Parallèle entre Cuba et quelques colonies Anglaises. Par Saco. - Cuba; with Notices of Porto-Rico and the Slave-trade. By D. Turnbull. Lond., 8vo., 1840.—The Island of Cuba. By R. R. Madden. Lond., 1849.— Bell's Geography.
CUBA, a town of Portugal, in the prov. of Alemtejo, comarca and 12 m. NNE of Beja. Pop. 2,500.

CUBA, a township in Alleghany co., in the state of New York, U. S., 280 m. W by S of Albany, on the New York and Erie railway. Pop. 1,768.—Also a village in Clinton co., in the state of Ohio.—Also a v. in Fulton co., in Illinois.
CUBA (La), a town of Spain, in the prov. and 40 m. ENE of Teruel.
CUBA. See SANTIAGO-DE-CUBA.

CUBAGUA, or CUAGUA, a small uninhabited island to the S of Margarita, in N lat. 10° 50', W long. 64° 17'. It is quite overgrown with a kind of thistle covered with strong black thorns, which renders it almost impassable. It was formerly famous for its pearl-fishery. Its only inhabitants are rabbits, seafowl, and a small species of deer.

CUBATAO, a village of Brazil, in the prov. of Mato-Grosso, on the route from Cuiaba to Mato- Grosse.—Also a v. in the prov. of São-Paulo, at the foot of the Serra-do-C., a portion of the Serra-do- Mar, and on the road which leads across the serra to the uplands of São-Paulo, by a steep ascent over 4 m. of solid pavement, and in a zig-zag course of upwards of 180 angles. Its summit-level is 2,250 ft. above sea-level, at a point 30 m. distant from São- Paulo, over a country of which the prevailing declination, as shown by the course of the streams, is inland, though the highest point within the city has precisely the same alt.—Also a river in the prov. of Santa-Catharina, which descends from the serra of the same name, and has an E course of 25 m. to the Brito.--Also a smaller stream in the same prov., which flows into the sea in the comarca Do Norte.- Also a stream in the prov. of São-Paulo, which, descending from the Serra-do-C, flows into the bay of Paranagua. It is navigable for about 12 m.
CUBBERLY, or COBERLY, a parish in Gloucestershire, 4½ m. SSE of Cheltenham. Area 3,800 acres. Pop. 231.
CUBBINGTON, a parish in Warwickshire, 5 m. ENE of Warwick, and SE of the river Avon. Area 1,780 acres. Pop. 830.
CUB CREEK, a village in Charlotte co., in the state of Virginia, U. S., 110 m. SW of Richmond.
CUBELLAS, a town of Spain, in Catalonia, in the prov. and 20 m. ENE of Tarragona, on the l. bank of the Foix. Pop. 1,000.
CUBELLS, a town of Spain, in Catalonia, in the prov. and 24 m. NE of Lerida, near the l. bank of the Segre.
CUBERT, a parish in the co. of Cornwall, 5 m. WNW of St. Michael's, near Holywell bay. Area 2,320 acres. Pop. 368.
CUBIE'RES, a commune and village of France, in the dep. of Lozere, cant. of Blaymard. Pop. 1,201.
CUBILLAS-DE-SERRATO, a town of Spain, in Old Castile, prov. and 18 m. NE of Valladolid, on an affluent of the Pisuerga.
CUBILLOS, a town of Spain, in Leon, in the | height, and is about 90 m. in circumf. On the S and CUC prov. and 12 m. E of Villafranca, near the r. bank of the Sil.
CUBILLOS-DEL-ROXO, a town of Spain, in Old Castile, in the prov. and 40 m. N of Burgos.
CUBJAC, a commune and village of France, in the dep. of Dordogne, cant. of Savignac, on the r. bank of the Haute-Vezère. Pop. 1,085.
CUBLAC, a commune and village of France, in the dep. of Correze, cant. of Larche, near the r. bank of the Vezère. Pop. 1,084. Coal is wrought here.
CUBLEY, a parish in Derbyshire, 6 m. S of Ashbourne, on a branch of the river Dove. Area 2,410 acres. Pop. 425.
CUBLINGTON, a parish in Buckinghamshire, 6 m. NE of Aylesbury. Area 1,290 acres. Pop. 290.
CUBLIZE, a commune and village of France, in the dep. of Rhone, cant. of Thizy, on the r. bank of the Rhone, 18 m. W of Villefranche-sur-Saone. Pop. 3,096.
CUBY, a parish in the co. of Cornwall, 4 m. N of Tregony. Area 2,410 acres. Pop. 1,156.

CUBZAC, a commune and village of France, in the dep of Gironde, cant. of Saint-Andre-de-Cubzac, on the r. bank of the Dordogne, 8 m. NNE of Bordeaux. Pop. 1,038. Large quantities of wine are shipped on the river here, which is crossed by a vast suspension bridge, having a length of roadway of 1,800 French ft., and raised 120 ft. from the surface of the water, which elevation is gained by a viaduct on either side of 27 fine arches. This noble work was begun in 1835, and completed in 1839.
CUCHIVARA. See Purus.

CUCHULLIN HILLS, a remarkable range in the isle of Skye, presenting a "connected chain of craggy peaks, whose fantastic outlines, in certain positions, may perhaps vie with any in the world." They commence at the head of Scavig-bay, and run NW, enclosing the valley of Loch Coruisk, between the principal chain on the W, and a secondary chain on the E [see Corusk]; and then turn NE, enclosing in like manner the valley of Hart-o-Corry. Professor Forbes estimates the alt. of Scuir-na-Gillean, at the NE extremity of the range, at between 3,200 and 3,220 ft.; and that of Bruch-na-Fray, to the W of Scuir-na-Gillean, at about 40 ft. lower. Their mass is composed of hypersthene rock, which is a compound of the mineral of that name with felspar.
CUCKFIELD, a market-town and parish in the co. of Sussex, 30 m. ENE of Chichester, in the vicinity of the London and Brighton railway. Area of p. 10,500 acres. Pop. 3,444.
CUCKLINGTON-WITH-CLAPTON-FORMS, a parish in Somersetshire, 3 m. ESE of Pincanton. Area 1,780 acres. Pop. 339.

CUCKNEY, a parish in Nottinghamshire, 5 m. S by W of Worksop. In 1838 there were two cotton-mills, employing 210 hands. Area, including the townships of Holbeck, Langwith, and Norton, with the hamlet of Balgh, 5,510 acres. Pop. 1,697.
CUCKOOVILLE, a village in Louisa co., in the state of Virginia, U. S., 47 m. NW of Richmond.
CUCUINA, a river of Venezuela, in the delta of the Orinoco, which inosculates with the Cano Pedernales, Cano Macarao, and Cano Soldado.
CUCULLO, a town of Naples, in Abruzzo-Ultra 2da, 8 m. W of Sulmona. Pop. 1,200.
CUCURRON, a commune and town of France, in the dep. of Vaucluse, cant. of Cadenet, 7 m. SSE of Apt. Pop. 2,082.

CUCUTA (VALLEYS OF), a district of New Grenada, in the prov. of Pamplona, intersected by the rivers Sulia, Pamplona, and Tachica, and containing a pop. of about 30,000. The district is divided into a series of valleys by ranges of hills of inconsiderable E is the Cordillera, here of small comparative elevation; the luxuriant forest which borders the Sulia encloses the valleys on the N and W. The soil is prevailingly sandy, and the hills are without verdure; but the valleys are wonderfully fertile. The coffee tree is cultivated on the higher grounds, and the sugar-cane, indigo, and cacao tree flourish in the valleys. The produce of the plantations is sent to Maracaybo, by the Sulia, in bongos or canoes 30 ft. long, and 6 ft. broad, often made out of a single tree. Part of the produce is sent down the Magdalena to Monpox and Cartagena. Cattle are few in number, but are supplied from the plains of Varinas and Casanare.

CUCUTA, or ROSARIO-DE-CUCUTA, the cap. of the above district, 28 m. NNE of Pamplona, is a neat well-built town. It is of small extent, but has obtained historical celebrity as having been the place in which the first general congress of Colombia as held, and as having been fixed upon by the fi ::ers of Colombian independence as the site on which, "in happier days," should rise the future cap. of Colombia, "the city of Bolivar."

CUDDALORE, a town of Hindostan, in the Carnatic, on the W shore of the bay of Bengal, in N lat. 11° 44′, 86 m. SSE of Madras. It is built on both sides of the Pennar river; and its position would probably have been found more favourable than that of Madras for the chief settlement of the British on the coast of Coromandel. The English East India company obtained a grant of it from the rajah of Gingee in 1681, for the construction of a factory; and the encouragement thereby given to manufacturers and traders so greatly increased the town in the course of a cent., that it was thought necessary to fortify it. It was taken by the French in 1758, but was recaptured by Sir Eyre Coote. In 1782 it was again taken by the French, who strongly fortified it, and kept possession of it till the termination of the war in 1783. The fort is described as being at that period a quadrangle of unequal sides, with an indifferent rampart and ditch, an outwork advanced from the NE angle, and a bastion covering each of the other angles. The country in the vicinity suffered much during the wars with Hyder Ali, and most of the inhabitants fled. It is now comprehended in the collectorship of Arcot. The factory still exists, and is the residence of the collector. A paper-manufactory has been established in this town. C. is one of the principal stations where soldiers are placed who choose to remain in India after having served out their time or become invalided. The comparative salubrity and coolness of its climate has occasioned its selection for this purpose. CUDDAPAH, KUDAPA, or KIRPA, an extensive district of Hindostan, in the Balaghant territory, between the parallels of 13° and 16° N. Area 12,753 sq. m. It was formerly governed by a nabob, who received his appointment from the court of Delhi, but subsequently fell into the hands of the nizam of the Deccan, by whom, in the year 1800, it was ceded to the British. The pop. was estimated in 1828-9 at 981,562; in 1837 at 1,063,164. The revenue in 1817 was about £300,000; in 1836 7, £233,406. Except on the hills, the country is fruitful, producing all kinds of grain, sugar, and cotton, and is yearly improving under the care of a fostering government. In some districts, saltpetre abounds. Its principal towns are Cuddapah, Wandicotta or Ganjcotta, Sidhaut, Panganur, and Cummum.

CUDDAPAH, a town and fortress of Hindostan, the cap. of the above district, is situated in N lat. 14° 32', E long. 78° 54', on a stream of the same name, at an alt. of 507 ft. above sea-level. A considerable quantity of sugar is manufactured in the vicinity. This CUE place is mentioned in history as early as 1687, as the residence of a nabob, under the kings of Golconda; and on the conquest of that country by the Moguls, the same officer was confirmed in his appointments by Aurungzebe. The nabob of C. assassinated the nizam of Nasir-Jung in 1750: since that period the authority of the nabob has been gradually curtailed or abolished; and this town is now the residence of the British officers of the collectorate. - About 7 m. NE of this town are celebrated diamond-mines.
CUDDESDEN, a parish in Oxfordshire, 64 m. ESE of Oxford. Area, with the chapelry of Wheatley, and hamlets of Chippinghurst and Denton, 2,700 acres. Pop. 1,483.

CUDDINGTON, a parish in Buckinghamshire, 51 m. WSW of Aylesbury. Area 980 acres. Pop. 626.—Also a township in the p. of Waverham, co. of Chester, 43 m. WSW of Northwich, on a branch of the Mersey. Area 990 acres. Pop. 253. - Also a township in the p. of Malpas, co. of Chester, 2 m. WSW of Malpas. Area 1,260 acres. Pop. 240.- Also a parish in the co. of Surrey, 1 m. NNE of Ewell. Area 1,850 acres. Pop. 158.

CUDGEEGONG, a river of New South Wales, which takes its rise on the W side of Mount Coriaday, in the SE corner of Phillip co.; runs along the S and SW border of that co., separating it from Roxburgh and Wellington counties; and receiving in its course Lawson, Pipe-clay, and Emu creeks. After the junction of the latter, it bends WSW through the last-mentioned co., and unites on its W frontier with the Macquarie.
CUDHAM, a parish in the co. of Kent, 7 m. SSE of Bromley. Area 5,930 acres. Pop. 776.
CUDILLERO, a town of Spain, in the Asturias, on the coast, in the prov. of Oviedo. It has a port.
CUDOS, a commune of France, in the dep. of Gironde, cant. of Bazas. Pop. 1,165.
CUDREFIN, a village of Switzerland, in the cant. of Vaud, on the r. bank of the lake of Neufchatel. Pop. 636.

CUDWORTH, a parish in Somersetshire, 3 m. SE of Ilminster. Area 1,070 acres. Pop. 155.— Also a township in the p. of Royston, W. R. of Yorkshire, 31 m. NE by E of Barnesley, intersected by the York and Derby railway. Area 1,520 acres. Pop. 552.
CUELLAR, a town of Spain, in Old Castile, in the prov. and 32 m. N of Segovia. Pop. 3,000.

CUE'NCA, a province of Spain, comprising the E portion of New Castile. The old prov. of this name is situated between N lat. 39° 2' and 41° 12'; and is bounded on the N by the provs. of Guadalaxara and Soria; on the E by Aragon and Valencia; on the S. by Murcia and La-Mancha; and on the W by Toledo and Madrid. Its length from N to S is 165 m.; its medium breadth 93 m. The new prov., formed in 1822, comprises the greater part of the old prov., with some annexations from Guadalaxara, Chinchilla, Valencia, and Calatayud; and is bounded on the N by the prov. of Guadalaxara; on the E by Teruel and Valencia; on the S by Chinchilla and Ciudad Real; and on the W by Toledo and Madrid. The area of the new prov. is estimated at 11,304 sq. m. A branch of the Iberian mountain-chain traverses the prov. from N to S, between Aragon and Castile, under the names of the Sierra-de-Molina and the Sierra-d'Albaracin; and diverging to the SW, takes the name of Sierra-de-Cuénça. The Toledos are a continuation of this chain, and occupy the W portion of the prov. The level districts are principally towards the SE, along the borders of the Tagus, which has its source among the mountains of this prov. The middle portions of C. are watered by the Jucar, and its affluent the Cabriel, by the Giquela, and the Zancara. The climate is very mild, and throughout the country is considered extremely healthy; but the cold of winter is intense in the mountainous districts. The soil in the plains and valleys seems to be a mixture of sand and calcareous matter. Not above a sixth part of the surface is under cultivation, the rest being used as pasture-land, upon which great numbers of cattle, horses, sheep, mules, and pigs are reared. Corn, maize, rye, barley, and oats sufficient for the supply of the inhabitants are raised; the vine is cultivated to advantage; and bees and silk-worms are extensively reared. The mountains are covered with forests of valuable building-timber. The mineral productions are iron, copper, alum, jasper, rock crystal, salt, and saltpetre. There are several chalk-pits, and some mineral springs; among the latter, the most famed are those of Sacedon and Solan-de-Cabras. Manufactories of glass, paper, coarse cloth, and soap, exist in this prov. Wood, iron, wine, hemp, cattle, and wool, are the chief articles of commerce.—This prov., in proportion to its extent, is the most thinly peopled district in all Spain. In 1797 the pop. was estimated at 294,290; in 1834 at only 234,582. It is subdivided into the 9 partidos of Belmonte, Canete, C., Huete, Motilla-del- Palancar, Priego, Requena, San-Clemente, and Tarancon; and comprises 333 pueblos.

CUENÇA, the capital of the above prov., is seated near the centre of the prov., on an elevated plateau, between two lofty mountains at the base of which flow the Jucar and the Guecar. The latter is here spanned by a stone bridge 160 ft. high and 300 long. The city is surrounded by high walls. Its streets are irregularly built, and very steep. It is a bishop's see, and is one of the cities which sent deputies to the old cortes of Castile. The cathedral and other ecclesiastical edifices are handsome. Pop. 8,670.

CUENÇA, a province of Ecuador, in the dep. of Assuay; bounded on the N by the prov. of Riobamba; on the E by that of Quixos and Macas; on majon. the S by Jaen-de-Bracamoros; and on the W by Guayaquil. It is intersected by mountains and valleys, and is watered by several rivers, of which the most considerable is the Paute. The climate is mild and salubrious; and the territory is productive in grain, sugar, cotton, and bark. Large quantities of cochineal are gathered here; and mines of copper, mercury, sulphur, gold and silver exist; but are not worked. C. is celebrated for its cotton fabrics, tapestry, and a kind of drugget or red baize.

CUENÇA, the cap. of the above prov., is situated in S lat. 2° 53′ 3″, 85 m. SSW of Quito, in the picturesque vale of Yunquilla, between three little rivers, and about 8,278 ft. above the level of the sea. It is a town of considerable extent but mean appearance, the houses being built of brick and very low. The streets are long and regular; but, with the exception of the Jesuits' convent, none of the public edifices are worthy of remark. C. was constituted a bishopric in 1786. The cathedral, 2 parish-churches, hospital, and convents are in the plainest style. The commerce of this place is chiefly in grain, bark, and the productions of the neighbouring mountains. Here are sugar-refining houses, the manufacture of hats has been introduced, and a kind of cheese is made and exported to Lima and other places. The pop. has been estimated at 20,000, of whom 3,000 are Indians. At the S extremity of the city is the hill of Tarqui, which was fixed upon by Condamine, Bouguer, and Godin for establishing their meridian line in 1742. Quicksilver mines exist about 20 m. NE of C.
CUERDALE, a township in the p. of Blackburn, Lancashire, 3 m. E of Preston, on the E bank of the Ribble, and W of the Derwent. Area 500 acres. Pop. 106. II. * CUE
CUERDEN, a township in the p. of Leyland, in Lancashire, 5 m. NNW of Chorley, on the river Lostock. Area 930 acres. Pop. 573.
CUERDLEY, or CRUEDLEY, a township in the p. of Prescot, Lancashire, 41 m. WSW of Warrington, N of the Mersey, and near the Sankey canal. Area 1,420 acres. Pop. 221.

CUERNAVACA, a town of Mexico, in the intendancy and 17 m. S of Mexico, finely situated on a tongue of land forming a plateau of the Sierra Guichelaque, and jutting out into the lap of the valley, at an alt. of 5,500 ft. above the Pacific, and 4,400 ft. below the Cruz-del-Marques; and entrenched on three sides by vast gorges, in which matchless sterility is to be found blended with the most luxuriant vegetation of the tropics. Pop. about 10,000. It possesses a number of handsome buildings, including a large church and prison. It was the cap. of the Tlahuica nation; and in the vicinity is the celebrated fortified hill and palace of Xochicalco, or the 'House of Flowers.' The beautiful suburbs of C. are chiefly inhabited by Indians.
CUERNE, a commune of Belgium, in the prov. of West Flanders, 2 m. NE of Courtray, on the Lys. Pop. 2,490. It has manufactures of linen and cotton fabrics and distilleries.

CUERS, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of the Var, arrond. of Toulon-sur-Mer. The cant. comprises 4 com. Pop. in 1831, 9,048; in 1841, 8,609. The town, which is noted for its salubrity, is 12 m. NE of Toulon-sur-Mer. Pop. in 1841, 4,309. It has an active trade in wine, brandy, olive-oil, capers, and figs.

CUERVA, a town of Spain, in New Castile, in the prov. and 20 m. SW of Toledo, partido of Nova- Hermosa. Pop. 1,800. It has some manufactories of silk and common cloth.—Also a town of New Castile, in the prov. of Guadalajara and partido of Ta-
CUERVO, a town of Spain, in Aragon, in the prov. and 20 m. SW of Teruel, partido and 15 m. S of Albarracin, near the r. bank of the Turia.
CUES (Los), or AZTEQUE, a populous village of Mexico, in the intendancy and 60 m. N of Oaxaca. It is noted for an Azteque fortress and other ancient fortifications.
CUESTA (LA), a mountain-summit in the prov. of La Rioja, in the Buenos-Ayrean confederacy, in the Cordova range. Alt. 2,500 ft. above the surrounding plains.
CUEVA-CARDUL, a town of Spain, in Old Castile, in the prov. and 15 m. E of Burgos, on the r. bank of the Oca.
CUEVAS-PROBANCO, a town of Spain, in Old Castile, in the prov. and 39 m. NNE of Segovia, partido of Cuellar, on the r. bank of the Bolijas.
CUEVAS-DEL-VALLE, a town of Spain, in Old Castile, in the prov. of Avila, partido of Arenasde-San-Pedro. Pop. 707. It has a parish-church and custom-house.
CUEVAS-DE-VELASCO, a town of Spain, in New Castile, in the prov. and partido, and 36 m. WNW of Cuença.

CUEVAS-DE-VERA, a town of Spain, in Andalusia, in the prov. and 45 m. NE of Almeria, and partido of Vera, on the r. bank of the Cuevas or Almanzora, 6 m. NW of the entrance of that river into the Mediterranean. Pop., including that of the suburbs Alanchete, Barsaine, Cargacin, Comara, and Postilla, 10,140. It has a parish-church, several chapels, a school, &c. Fishing and the manufacture of saltpetre, with the working of the recently discovered mines in the environs, form the chief employments of the inhabitants.

CUEVAS-DE-VINROMA', a town of Spain, in Valencia, in the prov. and 27 m. N of Castellon-dela-Plana, partido of Albocacer, on the r. bank of the Seco, 10 m. NW of the confluence of that river with the Mediterranean, and on the road to Catalonia. Pop. 2,600. It has a parish-church, several convents, an hospital, and a custom-house.
CUFFIES, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Aisne, cant. of Soissons. Pop. 719. It has an extensive glass-work.
